coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots-loaded macrophages as a biomimetic delivery system for bioimaging and photodynamic therapy
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Drug Delivery Science and Technology
coredata.dc:creator: Kang, Sung Hun
Lee, Jung-Yup
Kim, Sung Kyun
Byun, Soo-Hwan
Choi, Il
Hong, Seok Jin
coredata.dc:description: Macrophages have been recommended for site-specific tumoral delivery of genes, drugs, or nanoparticles. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are a well-known fluorescence-based diagnosis and photodynamic therapy (PDT) agent. Herein, we determined the therapeutic efficacy of hybrid GQD-loaded macrophages in a 3D culture model and a xenograft mouse model of human lung cancer cells. We examined the PDT efficacy of hybrid GQD-loaded macrophages in both models using LED light. Cell viability assay showed that the LED light-based PDT induced cell death via GQDs. Reactive oxygen species and Ca2+ detection assays indicated that GQD-loaded macrophages induced apoptosis in the in vitro 3D culture model. In vivo fluorescence bioimaging and ex vivo fluorescence images of major organs (the kidney, liver, lung, spleen, and tumor) revealed a tumor-homing effect of GQD-loaded macrophages in the xenograft mouse model. Our results demonstrated that hybrid GQD-loaded macrophages are a promising biomimetic delivery system for bioimaging and cancer therapy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Macrophages
Photodynamic therapy
3D human lung cancer model

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In vitro photodynamic therapy study under co-culture with A-549 cells
Photodynamic treatment of an in vitro 3D lung cancer model
Biodistribution of GQD-loaded macrophages in a lung cancer xenograft model

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of the photo-absorption and fluorescence effects of GQDs. a) Schematic presentation of the synthesis of GQDs and the photo-absorption of GQDs under daylight and fluorescence. b) UV–visible spectrum and c) fluorescence spectrum of GQDs. d) FT-IR graph of l-glutamic acid and GQDs. Reproduced with permission [25]. 2022, Hallym University.

Preparation of GQD-loaded macrophages. a) Time-dependent holography image of GQD (green) uptake by macrophages for 3–30 h. b) 3D holography image of GQD (green)-loaded macrophages. c) UV–visible and d) PL spectrum for analysis of the photo-activity of GQD-loaded macrophages. e) GQD cellular uptake at different concentrations. Reproduced with permission [25]. 2022, Hallym University. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Photodynamic therapy efficacy of GQD-loaded macrophages in 3D human Lung cancer model. a) Scheme of the OncoCilAir™ 3D lung cancer culture model. b) The therapeutic efficacy of GQD-loaded macrophages was confirmed by time-lapse imaging of H&E-stained tissues (24, 48, and 72 h) and c) confocal image (160 min). The efficacy was compared with the control using the OncoCilAir™ 3D lung cancer model. d) Photo-induced ROS and e) Ca2+ detection images indicate that GQD-loaded macrophages induced ROS-based apoptosis in OncoCilAir™ (in vitro 3D human lung cancer model). (M-GQD: GQD-loaded macrophages; white arrows indicate the brightest fluorescent regions; scale bar: (b) 20 μm, (c) 10 μm, (d and e) 50 μm).

NIR fluorescence image. Representative in vivo NIR fluorescence image of xenograft tumor-bearing mice at 0- to 12-h post-injection of (Up) GQDs or (Bottom) GQD-loaded macrophages (N-number: 10; tumor cell line: A-549; color mapping type: red; white arrows indicate the brightest fluorescent regions). Reproduced with permission [25]. 2022, Hallym University. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Target delivery efficacy of GQD-loaded macrophages. a) NIR fluorescence image of major organs (the brain, heart, kidney, liver, lung, spleen, and tumor) at 1-, 6-, and 48-h post-injection of GQDs or GQD-loaded macrophages. b) Comparison of fluorescence intensity between GQDs and GQD-loaded macrophages at 6 and 48 h. Reproduced with permission (N-number: 10) [25]. 2022, Hallym University.
The fluorescence intensity of GQDs or GQD-loaded macrophages in major organs (the brain, heart, kidney, liver, lung, spleen, and tumor) at 6- and 48-h post-injection.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots-loaded macrophages as a biomimetic delivery system for bioimaging and photodynamic therapy

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Current affiliation: Department of Otolaryngology-Head and Neck Surgery, Kangbuk Samsung Hospital Sungkyunkwan University School of Medicine, Seoul, Republic of Korea.
These authors contributed equally to this study.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Macrophages have been recommended for site-specific tumoral delivery of genes, drugs, or nanoparticles. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are a well-known fluorescence-based diagnosis and photodynamic therapy (PDT) agent. Herein, we determined the therapeutic efficacy of hybrid GQD-loaded macrophages in a 3D culture model and a xenograft mouse model of human lung cancer cells. We examined the PDT efficacy of hybrid GQD-loaded macrophages in both models using LED light. Cell viability assay showed that the LED light-based PDT induced cell death via GQDs. Reactive oxygen species and Ca2+ detection assays indicated that GQD-loaded macrophages induced apoptosis in the in vitro 3D culture model. In vivo fluorescence bioimaging and ex vivo fluorescence images of major organs (the kidney, liver, lung, spleen, and tumor) revealed a tumor-homing effect of GQD-loaded macrophages in the xenograft mouse model. Our results demonstrated that hybrid GQD-loaded macrophages are a promising biomimetic delivery system for bioimaging and cancer therapy.

Owing to their stable photoluminescence, high biocompatibility, and low toxicity properties, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are suitable for nanomedical applications. Macrophages retained the fluorescent effect of GQDs and were delivered into tumor lesions in a xenograft mouse model. Additionally, we confirmed that GQD-loaded macrophages showed photodynamic therapeutic efficacy in a 3D human lung cancer model in vitro. We believe that our study demonstrates the feasibility of cell-based delivery systems for cancer theragnostic.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene nanomaterials, including graphene quantum dots (GQDs), have specific characteristics, such as various luminescence colors, excellent photostability, and good biocompatibility [1]. Owing to their advantages, GQDs can be used for nanomedical applications as alternatives to conventional semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), which contain hazardous heavy metals [2]. Fluorescent organic dyes and semiconductor QDs composed of heavy metals are widely used for visualizing living cells ex vivo and in vivo. However, the photobleaching of organic dyes and the toxicity of heavy metals hinder their applications. GQDs can overcome these limitations owing to their stable photoluminescence, high biocompatibility, and low toxicity [3,4].

Tumor-associated macrophages (TAMs) constitute a major cellular component in the non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) microenvironment. All stages of tumor progression are associated with TAMs. Tumor cells attract macrophages and other inflammatory cells in the pre-invasion stage by producing cytokines and exosomes. TAMs play a direct role in the immune evasion of NSCLC and support the growth, invasion, and metastasis of cancer cells. TAMs are classified into two main types based on their functions: M1 and M2 macrophages [5]. Lung cancer studies have demonstrated that TAMs express the M1 marker and that a macrophage phenotypic transition from M1 to M2 occurs during tumorigenesis [6,7].

TAMs are effective as carriers in cancer-targeted delivery systems because of several properties [8–10]. For example, isolating monocytes from peripheral blood is easier than isolating other cellular carriers; their phagocytic properties are suitable for drug or nanoparticle loading to macrophages, and they are usually present in the tumor microenvironment [11]. Macrophages with the M1 phenotype are generally considered suitable as carriers for nanoparticle delivery to tumor tissues. Their phagocytic potency is higher than that of other macrophages and they are not hindered by the tumor microenvironment, allowing them to inhibit tumor progression [5].

A three-dimensional (3D) microscope can be used to examine the interaction between nanomaterials and cells as well as the phagocytosis of nanoparticles by macrophages [12]. Holotomography, also known as 3D quantitative phase imaging, is a method for label-free, high-resolution imaging in living cells [13], which can overcome the limitations of using fluorescent dyes, such as morphological changes and phototoxicity.

Nanomaterials are rapidly emerging tools for therapeutic delivery and cancer treatment [14–16]. The site-specific delivery of nanoparticles is crucial for their clinical use, and the use of macrophages as delivery vectors for genes, drugs, or nanoparticles in tumors has been suggested. GQDs are well-known agents for fluorescence-based diagnosis and photodynamic therapy (PDT). Photodynamic therapy (PDT) has been introduced as an alternative treatment for cancer treatment. It has gained popularity in the medical field owing to its non-invasive, non-ablative, safe, and effective therapeutic effects. These light sources of phototherapy offer several advantages, including low cost, ease of use, and the ability to cover larger areas of the body. Additionally, LED light therapy generally has a minimal risk of side effects and complications, making it an attractive treatment option for a variety of conditions [17–19].

In this study, we synthesized GQDs via pyrolysis using l-glutamic acid to improve their biocompatibility, stability, and theragnostic efficacy. We performed cell imaging and fluorescence bioimaging assays using a 3D holotomography microscope to determine the therapeutic efficacy of hybrid GQD-loaded macrophages in a 3D cultured human lung cancer model and lung cancer xenograft mouse model.

l-glutamic acid was supplied by Tokyu Chemical Industry Co., Ltd. (Tokyo, Japan). Mouse macrophages (RAW 264.7; ATCC#TIB-71) were maintained in a high-glucose Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum, dimethyl sulfoxide, and 100 U/mL penicillin. The macrophages were cultured at 37 °C with 10% CO2. DMEM, fetal bovine serum, penicillin, and 0.05% Trypsin-EDTA were purchased from Gibco® by Life Technologies™. Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) was acquired from BioNieer (Daejeon, Korea). Dimethyl sulfoxide was purchased from VWR Life Science (Radnor, PA, USA).

GQDs were synthesized following a previously reported protocol by Nafiujjaman et al. [20]. l-glutamic acid (2 g) was added to a glass bottle and heated at 220 °C. When the solid l-glutamic acid liquified and turned brown, the reaction was terminated. Next, 10 mL of water was added to the l-glutamic acid solution. The mixture was then stirred for 30 min, cooled to room temperature, and centrifuged at 10,000×g for 30 min. The supernatant was lyophilized and stored. To characterize GQD synthesis, we utilized several tools, including Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR spectrometer; Cary 610/660; Agilent Technologies, Santa Clara, CA, USA) to analyze the pyrolysis deformation of l-glutamic acid and formation of the GQDs, dynamic light scattering (DLS; zetasizer Nano S90; Malvern Panalytical, Malvern, UK) at 25 °C to analyze the size of GQDs, and thermogravimetric analysis (TGA; PerkinElmer, Waltham, MA, USA) in an aqueous solution to analyze thermo-specific reactions.

Lipopolysaccharide was utilized to activate RAW 264.7 cells and convert them from monocytes to macrophages. Macrophages were seeded in a 50-mL conical tube at a density of 1 × 106 cells per 2 mL of DMEM solution. Lipopolysaccharide (2 g) was then added to the macrophage suspension solution. The cells are incubated with 1 mL of GQDs (10 mg/mL) for 24 h at 37 °C in 5% CO2. They were then rinsed thrice with PBS and spun at 1400 rpm to remove non-ingested GQDs. Finally, the GQD-loaded macrophages were analyzed using a UV–visible spectrophotometer (OPTIZEN IV; Mecasys Co. Ltd, Daejeon, South Korea), holographic microscope (HT-2H; Tomocube, Daejeon, South Korea), and fluorescence spectrometer (FS-2; SCINCO Co. Ltd, Seoul, South Korea).

We examined the 3D RI distribution and fluorescence image using a holotomography system (Tomocube HT-2; Tomocube, Inc.; Daejeon, Korea). 2D holographic images were used to reconstruct with 3D RI tomography. These 2D holographic images were acquired at various angles from 49 lights and 48 azimuthal symmetry directions. The illumination source is a diode-pumped solid-state laser (λ = 532 nm), and beams from the laser are split into two arms using a 2 × 2 fiber. When the beam is irradiated and passes through the cell, the diffracted light is collected by an objective lens (NA = 1.2, UPLSAP 60XW; Olympus, Tokyo, Japan), projected onto a COMOS image sensor (FL3-U3-13Y3MC; FLIR Systems, Wilsonville, OR, USA), and interfered with by a reference beam to create a spatially modulated hologram. A digital micro-mirror device was utilized to rapidly and precisely control the angle of the illumination [21]. Detailed information on the principle of holotomography, the optical setup, and the reconstruction algorithm is provided elsewhere [22–24].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro photodynamic therapy study under co-culture with A-549 cells
ce:section.ce:para: A-549 cells were seeded in 96-well plates (4 × 104 cells/well) and incubated at 37 °C in 5% CO2 for 24 h. Different quantities of GQD-loaded macrophages were then added to the cells. After 30 min of co-culture, the plates were exposed to 660 nm LED light (30 mW/cm2) for 2, 5, and 10 min. After incubation for 24 h, MTT solubilization solution (100 μL) was added to each well to induce the formation of formazan crystals. After that, the absorbance of the solution was measured at 570 nm using a microplate reader (Epoch; Biotek, Winooski, VT, US).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photodynamic treatment of an in vitro 3D lung cancer model
ce:section.ce:para: OncoCilAir™ is a 3D human lung cancer model comprising three different human components, including lung fibroblasts, bronchial cells, and lung cancer cell lines. GQD-loaded macrophages were co-incubated with OncoCilAir™ for 30 min and exposed to LED light at 660 nm (30 mW/cm2) for 10 min. Subsequently, the efficacy of the photodynamic treatment was confirmed using four methods: hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining, dead cell detection (propidium iodide) assay, reactive oxygen species (ROS) detection, and Ca2+ detection assays. To quantify ROS production, 10 μL of H2DCFDA (10 μM) was added to the co-culture, followed by incubation for 30 min in the dark. Green fluorescence was observed at an excitation wavelength of 485 nm.

Moreover, 10 μL of the FuRA-AM (10 g/L) stain was used to detect Ca2+, and the signal was observed at 543 nm (excitation wavelength) in OncoCilAir™. All fluorescence images were obtained using an air immersion 20x objective lens of fluorescence microscopy (I×73P2F; Olympus, Tokyo, Japan) at a 20-min interval (20–160 min). Additionally, 160 min after treating GQD-loaded macrophages with LED light, 10 μL of the propidium iodide (0.5 g/L) stain was added to detect cell death.

All animal experiments were performed under the guidelines of the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of the KNOTUS (18-KE-392). To measure the number of macrophages accumulated in each organ (the heart, brain, kidney, liver, spleen, and lung), macrophages were tagged with GQDs.

Balb/c nude mice (CAnN.Cg-Foxn1nu/CrljOri) were purchased at 6 weeks of age from Orient Bio Inc. (Seoul, Korea). Subcutaneous tumors were implanted by inoculating A549 cells (5.0 × 106 cells/0.2 mL/head) to one side of the mouse body. When the tumor volume was approximately 100–150 mm3, 1.0 × 107 GQD-loaded macrophages were intravenously injected into each mouse via the tail vein. To determine the biodistribution of GQD-loaded macrophages, full body fluorescence images were observed 0, 1, 6, 9, and 12 h after administration, under anesthesia.

For ex vivo imaging studies, the animals were sacrificed at 6 and 9 h post-injection, and the organs (the heart, brain, kidney, liver, spleen, lung, and tumor) were collected. Next, the organs were washed with PBS and fixed in a 4% paraformaldehyde solution. Finally, fluorescence from each organ was analyzed using the FOBI (Fluorescence-labeled Organism Bioimaging Instrument; NeoScience Co., Ltd., Korea).

Results obtained from three independent replicates are presented as mean ± standard deviation. ANOVA (GraphPad) was used to determine statistical significance among three or more groups, respectively. Statistical significance was set at P-value <0.05.

GQDs were synthesized by pyrolysis via carbonization of l-glutamic acid (Fig. 1 a). This process was initiated by heating l-glutamic acid at 220 °C. At this temperature, l-glutamic acid melted to form a colorless liquid; subsequently, it carbonized and turned brown after a certain period. This color change indicated the formation of GQDs, which were characterized using UV–visible spectroscopy and measuring fluorescence intensity (PL). In the UV–visible spectra, one absorption peak was detected at 320 nm, which was attributed to the π-π entrapping and transition of the excited state energy by the surface states, leading to strong emission (Fig. 1b). The GQDs exhibited a fluorescence peak at 450 nm, with approximately 24% quantum yield under a 365-nm wavelength (Fig. 1c). The FT-IR spectroscopy in Fig. 1d shows the characteristic molecular changes after GQD synthesis. The main characteristic peak of l-glutamic acid is significantly decreased at 2855 cm−1 (C–H stretching), 1690 cm−1 (C=O stretching), and 1523 cm−1 (-NH stretching of the amino group). Moreover, several new peaks appeared at 3408 cm−1 (-OH stretching), 1710 cm−1 (C=C stretching), and 1297 cm−1 (C–N stretching of the secondary aromatic amine). The aqueous diameter of the GQDs was 15–25 nm in the 100 μg/mL solution. In addition, the thermal stability and colloidal stability of l-glutamic acid and GQDs were determined by TGA analysis (Fig. S1). The l-glutamic acid exhibited a 12.65% weight loss at 200 °C and a 47.34% loss at the 220–300 °C temperature interval. However, GQDs exhibited only a 65.32% weight loss at the 150–300 °C temperature interval.

Furthermore, we prepared GQD-loaded macrophages. For the intracellular uptake of GQDs, macrophages were activated using lipopolysaccharide. Time-lapse images were taken to track the accumulation of GQDs in the macrophages for 30 h. The GQD accumulation time was deduced from the change in the fluorescence of macrophages. The phagocytosis of GQDs by macrophages was completed within 18 h, and GQD fluorescence was maintained even after 30 h (Fig. 2 a, Video S1). 3D holotomography confirmed that GQDs successfully entered the macrophages (Fig. 2b, Video S2).
Supplementary video related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jddst.2023.104620

The following are the supplementary data related to this article.
Multimedia component 1
Video S1. Time-dependent holotomography video of GQD (green)-loaded macrophages for 30 h;.
Multimedia component 1
Multimedia component 1
Video S2. Holotomography video of GQD (green)-loaded macrophages.
Multimedia component 1

The UV–visible spectrum of GQDs and GQD-loaded macrophages revealed the optical properties of GQDs in the intracellular environment. The absorbance peak (at 325 nm) and fluorescence of GQDs were maintained after phagocytosis, suggesting that the loading process did not interfere with the optical properties of GQDs (Fig. 2c and d). The absorbance value of different concentrations of GQDs and GQD-loaded macrophages showed good GQD-loading properties at a 0.25–2 mg/mL feed ratio, indicating that the 1 mg/mL feed ratio is optimal for future use (Fig. 2e).

GQDs showed excellent anticancer efficacy under LED irradiation (Fig. S2). The potency of GQDs was mediated through the PDT process and was maintained even after uptake by macrophages under the same conditions. As shown in Fig. 3 a, we investigated the PDT efficacy of GQD-loaded macrophages using an OncoCilAir™ 3D lung cancer model. H&E-stained images showed that GQDs and GQD-loaded macrophages completely destroyed the lung cancer tissue within 72 h (Fig. 3b). Confocal images of GQD-loaded macrophages showed a low proportion of dead cells after LED irradiation (Fig. 3c). This result indicates that PDT with GQD-loaded macrophages requires time to induce cancer death. The fluorescence intensities of ROS and Ca2+ indicated that GQD-loaded macrophages exhibited the same PDT efficacy (Fig. 3d and e). ROS and Ca2+ fluorescence increased up to 120 min and then decreased rapidly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biodistribution of GQD-loaded macrophages in a lung cancer xenograft model
ce:section.ce:para: We used the A549 xenograft mouse model to study the homing property of macrophages to lung cancer tissue. In vivo fluorescence images of GQD- or GQD-loaded macrophages in the tumor-bearing mice showed the progression of each sample in the mouse body from administration to elimination. GQD-loaded macrophages showed stronger fluorescence than naked GQDs. These findings reveal that macrophages robustly protect GQDs from the in vivo environment (Fig. 4 ).

Our study confirmed that macrophages effectively encapsulated GQDs, maintained the fluorescence effect of GQDs, and delivered GQDs to the target lesion. Macrophages can phagocytose substances through several mechanisms. Our previous studies have shown that gold nanoparticles are successfully localized in macrophages, demonstrating that hybrid biomimetic gold nano-macrophages are promising biocompatible carriers for targeted drug delivery and overcoming the drawbacks of fabricated nanomaterials [9,26]. We have also identified the interactions between graphene-based nano-composite and macrophages through a quantitative study of the refractive index distribution of GQD-loaded macrophages using 3D holotomography [12]. The homing properties of macrophages have been investigated in an ovalbumin-induced asthma mouse model in vivo, and the results revealed that macrophage mobility was enhanced in the severe asthma model compared with that in healthy control mice [12]. The present study showed that macrophages phagocytosed GQDs within 24 h and is the first to demonstrate this phenomenon over time using a 3D holotomography microscope (Fig. 2a and Video S1). Moreover, our study revealed that in A549 xenograft mouse models, biomimetic hybrid GQD-loaded macrophages migrated around the tumor, emitting strong fluorescence signals compared to free GQDs; this finding suggested the homing properties of macrophages to migrate to the tumor site in a mouse model of lung cancer.

Cell-based drug delivery systems have advantages such as limited immunogenicity, reduced cytotoxicity, enhanced bioavailability, prolonged circulation, and improved pharmacokinetics [27]. Macrophages are key immune cell-based target drug delivery vehicles, and there are several hypotheses regarding their phagocytosis and migration. Hypoxia can induce macrophage polarization in A549 lung cancer cells. Polarization of M1 macrophages under hypoxia modifies the proinflammatory microenvironment and induces a phenotypic change in macrophages from M1 to M2, which reverses the inhibitory effect of proinflammatory cytokines in the cancer milieu.Most chemokine receptors are present on the plasma membrane of macrophages, which directs macrophage migration toward the inflammatory or tumor locations [28]. Tumor associated macrophages (TAMs) are mostly recruited from the peripheral cancer tissues due to the specific chemotactic factors released from the tumor. Among them, C–C modified chemokine ligand 2 (CCL2) and other chemokines, such as CCL3, CCL4, CCL5, and CXCL12, are the most investigated factors that recruit monocytes and macrophages. The tumor microenvironment (TME) is also one of the most dominant factors attracting TAMs [29,30]. However, further research is needed to elucidate the cytokines, chemokines, and receptors involved in the phagocytosis and migration of macrophages. Moreover, although we revealed that the fluorescence diminished at 48 h in the tumor regions, the underlying mechanism remains unclear. Therefore, future experiments should analyze the metabolic profile in the distribution areas of the macrophages to understand the mechanisms underlying the diminished fluorescence in the tumor.

GQDs are derivatives of graphene, with a large surface area as well as good thermal and chemical stability. Despite their inherent advantages, most carbon-based materials are hydrophobic, which disrupts the biomedical application of GQDs. Therefore, many researchers have tried chemical or physical modifications, such as PEGylation, oxidation, and amination, to avoid the hydrophobic surface problem [2]. First, both top-down and bottom-up methodologies can be used to categorize the approaches to GQD synthesis. The top-down method employs the method of exfoliating the carbon source to a small size. This process has several problems, such as a complex reaction process, low product yield, low quantum yield, and concerns about cytotoxicity owing to the use of acid treatment in the purification process. The bottom-up method includes GQD synthesis from small carbon precursors using one simple step. However, it involves a complex purification process [20]. In the present study, we used the pyrolysis method using l-glutamic acid, which is one of the precursors of GQDs (Fig. 1). The l-glutamic acid-fabricated GQDs do not have hydrophobic surface problems owing to their hydrophilic carboxyl and amine groups [20]. The molecular characterization of l-glutamic acid and GQDs (Fig. 1 and Fig. S1) revealed that the main characteristic FT-IR peak of l-glutamic acid is significantly changed due to the carbonization process to convert the GQDs [31–33]. Moreover, l-glutamic acid exhibited a downward curve due to the pyrolysis reaction under thermal treatment (Fig. S1), which indicated that the melting process begins at 200 °C and is completed at 220 °C after a certain time. At 220 °C, the beginning of the second downward curve, a cross-linking-like thermal reaction begins [34]. However, GQDs exhibited weight loss between 150 and 300 °C. This phenomenon might be attributed to the loss of some carboxyl and amine groups. Moreover, GQDs show high colloidal stability at < 150 °C [33].

In a previous study, Shao et al. [35] reported that based on their fluorescence intensity, amino GQDs maintained their intracellular stability for 48 h. The authors explained that the amine group of GQDs increases fluorescence stability and signal-to-noise ratio. Therefore, the amine group of l-glutamic acid also helps increase the stability of macrophages [36]. In the present study, we used alveolar macrophages as tumor-homing carriers of GQDs. According to our results, GQDs show clear fluorescence in macrophages for 30 h (Fig. 2), indicating that their fluorescence is stable during lysosomal degradation; thus, GQDs can be used for biological imaging.

As a characteristic phenotype, phagocytes can take up bacteria, cellular debris, and particles via the energy-consuming process of phagocytosis. This phenomenon plays a major role in the response to and removal of foreign objects. Most nanomaterials, including GQDs, follow the same mechanisms of absorption and phagocytosis as conventional immunological pathogenic processing. Typical macrophage surface receptors for recognizing nanomaterials are Toll-like receptors, mannose receptors, scavenger receptors, and Fc receptors [37]. Although all of these receptors can participate in phagocytosis, comparative studies have shown that scavenger receptors require a significantly longer time for the internalization of nanoparticles than mannose and Fc receptors. These differences in uptake rates indicate that scavengers may be host receptors for GQD uptake into macrophages [38].

During PDT, ROS and cellular metabolism interference play an essential role in the killing of cancer cells. ROS, such as 1O2, are produced by a photosensitizer under illumination in the presence of oxygen. Among graphene-based nanocomposites, GQDs are considered great candidates for PDT agents owing to their nontoxicity, high water stability, and 1O2 generation properties under light irradiation [3]. Our results showed that the GQD-loaded macrophages successfully generated ROS in the 3D Human lung cancer model at 20–120 min (Fig. 3d). These exogenous ROS interventions can disrupt the endogenous antioxidant defense systems in cancers by regulating the signal transduction and major apoptotic pathways that are mediated by the mitochondria and the endoplasmic reticulum, which is the main regulated site during the ROS-based treatment process. These cell organelles are also involved in ROS production under endogenous homeostasis. Therefore, ROS levels decrease over time due to their disruptive effects on these cellular organelles [18,39].

Ca2+ ions are one of the most important secondary messengers in cells, playing an essential regulatory role in tumor cell proliferation and migration. Low cytoplasmic calcium concentration induces the anti-apoptotic ability of cancer cells [40]. Ca2+ is mainly present in the mitochondria, endoplasmic reticulum, and cytoplasm. Calcium ion balance is maintained in the intracellular environment. In the middle and late stages of tumorigenesis, mitochondrial calcium uniporter and sarcoplasmic reticulum Ca2+-ATPase, called Ca2+ channel proteins, play an important role in reducing calcium ion concentration in the cytoplasm [41]. The therapeutic strategy proposed in the present study may help increase intracellular Ca2+ concentration by attracting the mitochondria, further leading to cancer cell apoptosis. The release of Ca2+ from internal reserves in the endoplasmic reticulum and mitochondria, as well as the inflow of Ca2+ through ion channels, may explain the efficacy of PDT with GQD-loaded macrophages (Fig. 3e) [42]. However, although calcium overload has been confirmed to be associated with tumor death, the underlying mechanism remains unclear.In the future, we will perform further experiments to confirm the photodynamic therapeutic efficacy and safety of GQD-loaded macrophages in vivo. Moreover, factors that affect GQD stability other than the pH should be investigated.

In summary, our study evaluated the tumor-targeting properties of GQD-equipped macrophages as transporters in a classic NSCLC cell line model, that is, an A549 cell xenograft mouse model. Our results showed that alveolar macrophages protected GQDs from blood fluid conditions. GQDs loaded in macrophages exhibited stronger fluorescence intensity than free GQDs in vivo. Moreover, this study revealed that PDT using GQD-loaded macrophages was effective in tumor cells (A-549 cells) in vitro.

This research was supported by Hallym University Research Fund 2020 (HURF-2020-60) in the materials purchase and animal experiment. Additionally, this research was supported by Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF), funded by the Ministry of Education (NRF-2021R1I1A3054949) and Ministry of Science and ICT (NRF-2021R1F1A1063590) in the collection, analysis, and interpretation of data.

Sung Hun Kang: Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing –Original Draft, Jung-Yup Lee: Validation, Writing – Original Draft, Sung Kyun Kim: Resources, Writing – Review & Editing, Soo-Hwan Byun: Methodology, Writing – Review & Editing, Il Choi: Visualization, Project administration, Seok Jin Hong: Conceptualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no conflict of interest.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jddst.2023.104620.
